name: Panel data & causal inference
description: 15 interview-screen MCQs — panel data & causal inference. Each card explains the answer.

glossary.DiD: Difference-in-Differences — a quasi-experimental method comparing before/after changes in treated vs. control groups to estimate causal effects
glossary.RDD: Regression Discontinuity Design — a causal inference method exploiting a threshold rule in a running variable to identify local treatment effects
glossary.IV: Instrumental Variables — an estimation approach using exogenous instruments to recover causal estimates when regressors are endogenous
glossary.FE: Fixed Effects — a panel data estimator that removes time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity by demeaning within each entity
glossary.RE: Random Effects — a panel data estimator that treats entity effects as random draws, more efficient when those effects are uncorrelated with regressors
glossary.GMM: Generalised Method of Moments — an estimation framework choosing parameters to match sample moments to theoretical moment conditions
glossary.RCT: Randomised Controlled Trial — an experimental design in which units are randomly assigned to treatment and control groups
glossary.OLS: Ordinary Least Squares — a regression method that minimises the sum of squared residuals
glossary.2SLS: Two-Stage Least Squares — a two-step IV estimator projecting endogenous variables onto instruments before the second-stage regression

primer:
## Panel data & causal inference
**Logit/probit** model a 0/1 outcome with an S-shaped link (coefficients act on the log-odds; compute marginal effects for probabilities). **Fixed effects** sweep out time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity using within-entity variation; **random effects** is more efficient *only if* those effects are uncorrelated with the regressors (Hausman test). Causal designs: **DiD** (parallel trends), **RDD** (jump at a cutoff), **IV** (as-if-random variation), randomisation (balances confounders in expectation). Beware confounding, reverse causality and selection.

cards.term: Logit and probit models are used when the dependent variable is:
choices: Continuous and unbounded
An unbounded integer count
A non-stationary time series
A strictly positive duration
Binary (a 0/1 outcome)
cards.definition: Logit and probit map a linear index through an S-shaped link (logistic CDF or normal CDF) so fitted probabilities stay in [0,1], which is exactly what a 0/1 outcome requires. A continuous, unbounded variable would call for [[OLS|ordinary linear regression]], not a bounded-probability link. An unbounded integer count is the domain of Poisson or negative-binomial models, a non-stationary time series calls for differencing or cointegration, and a strictly positive duration is handled by survival models such as Weibull or Cox; none of these are binary-choice models.

cards.term: In a logit model, the raw coefficient on a regressor gives:
choices: The effect on the log-odds of the outcome
The marginal effect evaluated at the sample mean
The change in fitted probability per unit
The variable's contribution to the model R-squared
The elasticity of the predicted outcome
cards.definition: Because logit applies a nonlinear logistic link, the raw coefficient measures the effect on the log-odds (the logit), not a direct probability change. It is not the marginal effect at the sample mean nor the per-unit change in probability: those require evaluating the slope of the nonlinear curve at chosen covariate values, since the probability impact depends on where you sit on the S-curve. It is not a contribution to model R-squared, which is a goodness-of-fit notion unrelated to a single coefficient, and it is not an elasticity, which would be a percentage-on-percentage response rather than an effect on log-odds.

cards.term: A fixed-effects (within) estimator in panel data controls for:
choices: Time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity
Common shocks shared across all entities
Classical measurement error in regressors
Heteroskedasticity in the panel errors
Reverse causality from outcome to regressor
cards.definition: The within (fixed-effects) estimator demeans each entity over time, so anything constant within an entity drops out, removing time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity such as innate ability or fixed firm culture. It does not absorb common shocks shared across all entities (those need time dummies, not entity demeaning), nor does it fix classical measurement error or heteroskedasticity, which require [[IV|instruments]] or robust/clustered standard errors. It also does not cure reverse causality: a time-varying confounder or simultaneity still biases the within estimator. Compare with [[RE]], which is more efficient when the entity effects are uncorrelated with regressors.

cards.term: The key difference between random effects and fixed effects is that random effects assume:
choices: The entity effects are uncorrelated with regressors
The data have only a single cross-sectional dimension
The entity effects are correlated with the regressors
The idiosyncratic panel errors are heteroskedastic
There are no entity-specific effects present at all
cards.definition: Random effects treats the entity-specific effects as random draws uncorrelated with the regressors, which is precisely the assumption that lets it pool between- and within-variation efficiently. If that orthogonality holds, random effects is more efficient than fixed effects; if it fails, only fixed effects stays consistent. The option saying the effects are correlated with the regressors describes the fixed-effects situation, the opposite case. The claims about a cross-sectional-only dimension, heteroskedastic errors, or no entity effects miss the point: both estimators use panel data and assume such effects exist; the sole distinction is whether those effects correlate with the regressors.

cards.term: The Hausman test in panel data helps you choose between:
choices: Logit and probit binary-choice link functions
Autoregressive and moving-average models
OLS and two-stage least-squares estimators
Fixed-effects and random-effects estimators
Value-at-risk and expected-shortfall measures
cards.definition: The Hausman test compares the [[FE|fixed-effects]] and [[RE|random-effects]] estimators: under the random-effects assumption both are consistent but random effects is efficient, while under correlation only fixed effects is consistent, so a large divergence signals that random effects is biased. It is not a tool for picking between logit and probit links, AR and MA models, [[OLS]] and [[2SLS]], or VaR and expected shortfall. The intuition is that it directly tests the orthogonality condition by checking whether switching estimators meaningfully changes the coefficients.

cards.term: Difference-in-differences estimation relies crucially on the assumption of:
choices: Fully random assignment of the treatment
Parallel pre-treatment trends across groups
Normally distributed regression error terms
The complete absence of any control group
Stationarity of the measured outcome series
cards.definition: Difference-in-differences subtracts each group's own before/after change and compares them, so it requires parallel trends: absent treatment, treated and control groups would have evolved along the same path, letting any post-treatment divergence be attributed to the treatment. It does not require random assignment, which is what makes DiD quasi-experimental rather than an [[RCT]], and it does need a control group, so 'no control group' is wrong. Normally distributed errors and stationary outcomes are not the identifying assumption; the entire credibility of DiD rests on the counterfactual common trend.

cards.term: Regression discontinuity designs identify causal effects by:
choices: Comparing units on either side of a cutoff
Randomly assigning units to the treatment arm
Differencing the panel data twice over time
Demeaning each entity with fixed effects
Instrumenting with lagged regressor values
cards.definition: [[RDD]] exploits a threshold rule in a running variable: units just below and just above the cutoff are nearly identical except for treatment, so the jump in the outcome at the cutoff identifies a local causal effect. It does not rely on random assignment (that is an [[RCT]]), on double differencing (that is [[DiD]]), on entity demeaning (fixed effects), or on a lagged-variable instrument ([[IV]]). The intuition is local as-good-as-random variation: assignment is deterministic in the running variable, but who lands marginally on each side of the cutoff is essentially noise.

cards.term: A natural experiment is valuable for causal inference because it:
choices: It requires no identifying assumptions at all
It gives variation unrelated to confounders
It removes heteroskedasticity in the errors
It eliminates the need for any observed data
It guarantees a high overall model R-squared
cards.definition: A natural experiment supplies variation in treatment generated by some external event or rule that is plausibly unrelated to confounders, approximating randomisation the researcher did not control. Quasi-experimental designs such as [[IV]], [[DiD]], and [[RDD]] all rely on such naturally occurring variation. It is not assumption-free: the as-if-random claim is itself an identifying assumption that must be defended. It does not remove heteroskedasticity, does not eliminate the need for data, and does not guarantee a high R-squared; fit and identification are separate matters. The value is precisely that the assignment mechanism mimics an [[RCT]] without deliberate randomisation.

cards.term: Generalized Method of Moments (GMM) estimation is based on:
choices: Minimising the model's R-squared
Differencing the series until stationary
Maximising the likelihood directly
Matching sample to theoretical moments
Bootstrapping the regression residuals
cards.definition: [[GMM]] estimates parameters by choosing them to make sample moments match the theoretical moment conditions implied by the model (e.g. that instruments are uncorrelated with the error), minimising a weighted quadratic distance between the two. It is not about minimising R-squared, differencing to stationarity, maximising a likelihood (that is ML, which needs a full distributional assumption GMM avoids), or bootstrapping residuals. The unifying intuition is that [[OLS]], [[IV]], and [[2SLS]] are all special cases of GMM with particular moment conditions and weighting matrices.

cards.term: Correlation does not imply causation chiefly because of:
choices: Heteroskedasticity in the regression errors
Rounding error in the recorded measurements
Non-normality of the regression error term
Confounding, reverse causality, or selection
Insufficiently large estimation sample sizes
cards.definition: Correlation need not be causation because an association can be produced by a confounder driving both variables, by reverse causality where the outcome affects the supposed cause, or by selection into the sample, all alternative explanations for the same pattern. Heteroskedastic errors, rounding error, and non-normal errors are issues of efficiency or inference, not of whether a relationship is causal. Small samples merely add noise; even an infinite sample with a precisely estimated correlation can still be entirely non-causal if a common cause is present.

cards.term: In a randomized controlled trial, randomization is what allows a causal interpretation because it:
choices: It removes measurement error in the outcomes
It increases the effective estimation sample size
It eliminates the need for any control group
It guarantees normally distributed outcome data
It makes treatment independent of confounders
cards.definition: Randomisation makes treatment status statistically independent of all confounders on average, so treated and control groups are comparable in expectation and the difference in outcomes estimates the causal effect. It does not remove measurement error, which is a property of how variables are recorded, nor does increasing sample size alone confer causal validity (a large biased study is still biased). It does not eliminate the need for a control group, the comparison group is what randomisation makes valid, and it does not guarantee normality; balance of confounders, not any distributional shape, is the source of causal interpretation.

cards.term: The 'selection bias' problem in observational studies refers to:
choices: Selecting too few regressors for the fitted model
Ordinary random sampling variability in the data
Choosing an incorrect functional form for the model
Units sorting into treatment on outcome traits
Setting the wrong significance threshold for a test
cards.definition: Selection bias arises when units sort into treatment based on characteristics that are themselves related to the potential outcomes, so a naive treated-versus-untreated comparison mixes the true treatment effect with pre-existing differences. Causal methods such as [[IV]] and [[DiD]] are designed precisely to overcome this problem. It is not about including too few regressors, ordinary sampling variability, choosing the wrong functional form, or setting the wrong significance threshold; those are specification or inference concerns. The core intuition is that when assignment is correlated with potential outcomes, who got treated is informative about what would have happened anyway, contaminating the estimate.

cards.term: A count outcome (e.g., number of defaults per month) with no natural upper bound is often modelled with:
choices: A linear probability model on the counts
A probit binary-choice model on the counts
An ARIMA model fitted to the raw counts
A Poisson or negative-binomial regression
A binary logit model applied to the counts
cards.definition: An unbounded, non-negative count is well suited to Poisson regression, with negative binomial as the extension when the variance exceeds the mean (overdispersion). A linear probability model, probit, and logit are for binary outcomes, not counts, and would not respect non-negativity or the integer support. Fitting an ARIMA model to the raw counts treats them as a continuous series and ignores the discrete, non-negative count structure, which is exactly what the count-data models are built to honour.

choices: Many variables at a single fixed point in time
One single entity tracked over many periods
A set of one-shot cross-sectional snapshots
Multiple entities over multiple time periods
Only aggregated national-level time series
cards.definition: Panel (longitudinal) data follow multiple entities across multiple time periods, giving both a cross-sectional and a time dimension. Many variables at one point in time is a single cross-section; one entity tracked over many periods is a single time series; one-shot snapshots or aggregated national series likewise lack the combined entity-and-time structure. The intuition is that observing the same units repeatedly lets you net out unobserved heterogeneity a single cross-section cannot, because each entity serves as its own control over time.

cards.term: Compared with cross-sectional data, a key advantage of panel data is the ability to:
choices: Avoid every possible form of estimation bias at once
Remove the need for instruments entirely
Guarantee stationarity of the observed data
Strip out heteroskedasticity automatically
Control for unobserved time-invariant effects
cards.definition: Relative to a cross-section, panel data let you control for unobserved, time-invariant individual effects by exploiting repeated observations of the same entities. It does not avoid all bias (time-varying confounders and simultaneity remain), does not remove the need for [[IV|instruments]] when endogeneity is present, does not guarantee stationarity, and does not automatically strip out heteroskedasticity, which still calls for robust or clustered standard errors. The intuition is that within-entity variation differences away fixed traits, attacking a major source of omitted-variable bias rather than every statistical problem at once.
